title: Chocolate éclairs
subtitle: Choux pastry fingers filled with vanilla cream and dipped in chocolate ganache
excerpt: Hollow choux pastry fingers baked until deep gold and crisp, filled with lightly sweetened whipped cream, and dipped in a dark chocolate ganache glaze. Makes about 12.
sourceNotes: The éclair, a finger of choux pastry filled with cream and glazed with chocolate, is documented in French patisserie guides from the mid-nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), includes the chocolate éclair among the standard patisserie petit-fours. The form is documented in British household baking guides from the early twentieth century as a tea-time pastry.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unfilled baked éclairs. Defrost at room temperature, refresh at 180°C fan for 5 minutes. Fill and glaze just before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The shells can be baked a day ahead and kept in a tin. Fill and glaze on the day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 210°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A ganache glaze is made by heating cream until it just steams and barely shows bubbles at the edges — this is scalded, not boiled. Boiled cream can split the fat from the water in ways that dull the gloss and alter the texture. Pour the scalded cream over chopped chocolate, rest for 30 seconds, then stir from the centre outwards. For a glaze that sets at room temperature, let it cool to 28–30°C before dipping. The butter added at the end increases the sheen.

content.text: Éclairs and profiteroles use the same choux paste, piped differently. Éclairs are piped into 10 cm fingers and demand a steadier hand, but the baking rules are the same: deep gold, hollow when tapped, pierce immediately. The
glaze sets to a firm, glossy coat if you let it cool slightly before dipping.
Make the choux and bake — 30–35 minutes

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} (pastry amount) with the {{sea-salt-fine}} and 240 ml cold water in a saucepan. Bring to a rolling boil. Remove from the heat and beat in the {{plain-flour}} all at once until the dough pulls away from the sides. Dry out over the heat for 1 minute. Cool for 5 minutes, then beat in the {{eggs}} gradually until the paste is glossy and drops reluctantly from the spoon. Pipe 10 cm fingers onto lined baking trays, spacing well apart. Bake for 30–35 minutes until deep gold. Pierce each shell at one end, then return to the switched-off oven for 3 minutes to dry. Cool completely on a rack.

Whip the {{double-cream}} (filling amount) with the {{icing-sugar}} and {{vanilla-extract}} to soft peaks. Split each éclair lengthways or pipe cream through the pierced end. For the glaze, heat the {{double-cream}} (glaze amount) until just steaming. Pour over the {{dark-chocolate}} and rest 30 seconds, then stir smooth. Stir in the {{unsalted-butter}} (glaze amount). Cool to 30°C. Dip the top of each filled éclair into the ganache. Set on a rack for 5 minutes before serving.
